The temple contains 104,000 square feet of floor space with 4 instruction rooms, 8 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry. Its floor plan was later used for the Mount Timpanogos Utah Temple.

This was one of only two temples dedicated by President Howard W. Hunter during his brief presidency. The dedicatory sessions drew 201,655 members, the largest attendance in Church history at that time, while 870,361 visitors toured during the 43-day public open house.

In 2016, lightning struck the Angel Moroni statue atop the temple, requiring its replacement. The temple serves as the first temple in Davis County and continues to serve members throughout the region.

name: Columbia South Carolina Temple

facts: Second temple built in Chile, serving members in southern Chile and becoming a significant landmark in the region

Underwent two major renovations resulting in rededications in 2002 and 2016, with the 2016 rededication performed by Dieter F. Uchtdorf, a German native and apostle

Contains two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry, serving Latter-day Saints throughout Germany and surrounding areas

name: Fresno California Temple
address: 6290 N Valentine Ave, Fresno, California 93711, United States
facts: The Fresno California Temple was the fourth temple built in California and the first in the Central Valley region
The temple is built with white Sierra granite quarried from Raymond, California, giving it a distinctive regional character
The temple sits on a 2.34-acre site at an elevation of 319 feet and has a total floor area of 10,850 square feet
Features a single attached spire topped with an Angel Moroni statue, two instruction rooms in a two-stage progressive ordinance format, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry
From announcement to dedication took only 15 months, reflecting the rapid construction timeline common to temples built during President Gordon B. Hinckley's administration
The temple serves members in California's Central Valley, a region with deep LDS history dating back to the sailing of the ship Brooklyn to San Francisco Bay in 1846

name: Fukuoka Japan Temple
address: 9-15 Hirao Johsui Machi, Chuo-ku, Fukuoka-shi, Fukuoka 810-0029, Japan
facts: Second temple built in Japan, following the Tokyo Japan Temple, and the 88th temple dedicated worldwide
Located on the southern island of Kyushu, built into a hillside near the Fukuoka Municipal Zoo and Botanical Garden at an elevation of 80 feet (25 meters)
Features a total floor area of 10,700 square feet (994 square meters) on a 1.25-acre (0.5 hectare) site, with a two-story design and single attached spire
Exterior finish uses Empress White and Majestic Grey granite imported from China, topped with a gold-leafed angel Moroni statue added on November 8, 1999
Contains two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive ordinance rooms), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry
The temple's construction and announcement rekindled activity among inactive Church members in the Fukuoka area
Public open house attracted over 4,800 visitors during its two-day run from June 1-3, 2000
First of four temples dedicated by President Gordon B. Hinckley during his June 2000 Asian/Pacific dedication tour

name: Gilbert Arizona Temple
address: 3301 S Greenfield Rd, Gilbert, Arizona 85297, United States

timeline.by: Thomas S. Monson (prayer read by Henry B. Eyring)

facts: The Gilbert Arizona Temple is the 142nd dedicated temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
The temple features a single attached central spire topped with a statue of the angel Moroni, standing at a total height of 195 feet (59.4 meters)
Total floor area of 85,326 square feet (7,927 square meters) includes 3 instruction rooms, 7 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
The temple sits on 15.38 acres (6.2 hectares) at an elevation of 1,269 feet (387 meters)
During the 28-day public open house from January 18 to February 15, 2014, a remarkable 407,020 guests toured the temple
The exterior features precast concrete panels and is surrounded by landscaping with fountains, shaded plazas, 10 tree types, 5 palm varieties, and 24 shrub and groundcover species
A 658-car parking lot serves temple patrons and visitors
President Thomas S. Monson stated about this temple: 'It is my personal priority to make sure members of the Church have access to the blessings of the temple'

name: Goiânia Brazil Temple

name: Grand Junction Colorado Temple
address: 678 Horizon Drive, Grand Junction, Colorado, United States
facts: The Grand Junction Colorado Temple is the 210th dedicated temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the third temple in Colorado.
The temple sits on a 7.93-acre site at an elevation of 4,692 feet (1,430 meters), with a total floor area of 29,630 square feet (2,753 square meters) and stands 118 feet 8 inches (36.2 meters) tall.
The exterior is finished with Portuguese Pedras Salgadas granite and features a single attached central spire. The exterior fence is Ameristar Montage Plus Majestic in bronze finish.

The temple incorporates design elements celebrating western Colorado's natural environment, with special emphasis on the claret cup cactus (Colorado's official cactus). Interior colors include crimson, amber, green, and pale pink inspired by local flora.
The temple features art glass by Holdman Studios with botanical themes, custom carpets and marbles reflecting regional landscapes, and African sapele hardwood millwork throughout.
The baptismal font railing design references the two rivers (Colorado and Gunnison) that converge at Grand Junction, giving the city its name.
Featured artwork includes paintings by Ken Stockton ('Joy in the Journey' and 'Rocks and Rills') and 'Sunset at San Ysidro' by Wilson Hurley.
The temple contains two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry. At the time of announcement, Colorado had over 150,000 Church members and 310 congregations.
The first Church mission in Colorado was established in 1896, more than 125 years before the Grand Junction temple was announced.

name: Grand Rapids Michigan Temple
address: 2400 Forest Hill Ave SE, Grand Rapids, Michigan 49546, United States

facts: Second temple in Michigan, following the Detroit Michigan Temple dedicated in 1999

name: Greenville South Carolina Temple
address: South of Independence Boulevard/Ponders Road and Roper Mountain Road, Greenville, South Carolina
facts: The Greenville South Carolina Temple will be the second temple built in South Carolina, joining the Columbia South Carolina Temple which has been operating since October 1999.
The temple will feature classical architectural elements including a pedimented entrance with columns, an ornamented steeple with white tower body, and a metal-clad spire.
The temple site encompasses 8.8 acres (3.6 hectares) at an elevation of 953 feet (291 meters), located south of Independence Boulevard/Ponders Road and Roper Mountain Road.
The single-story temple will have a total floor area of 18,850 square feet (1,751 square meters).
The temple will serve approximately 46,000 Latter-day Saints in South Carolina who are organized into 85 congregations across the state.

name: Guadalajara Mexico Temple

Located at 5,349 feet elevation on a 2.69-acre site in the Jardines Tepeyac neighborhood of Zapopan
The temple serves a district of 17 stakes and 9 districts in an area that grew from 4 branches to 8 stakes, serving the Guadalajara metropolitan area of approximately 4 million residents
It was the 105th operating temple of the Church and only took 11 months from groundbreaking to completion
At the groundbreaking ceremony, Elder Eran A. Call referenced President Lorenzo Snow's prophecy that temples would be established over every portion of the land of Zion

name: Guatemala City Guatemala Temple
address: 24 Avenida 2-20, Zona 15, Vista Hermosa 1, Guatemala City, Guatemala
facts: First temple built in Central America and third in a Spanish-speaking country
Constructed with natural white Guatemalan marble and features six detached spires with an Angel Moroni statue
Built during Guatemala's Civil War, with a dedication prayer specifically asking for national peace
Total floor area of 11,610 square feet with 4 instruction rooms, 3 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Located at an elevation of 5,148 feet (1,569 meters) in the Vista Hermosa neighborhood on a 1.4-acre site
John O'Donnal, who advocated for missionaries in Guatemala in 1946-1947, became the first temple president, and his wife Carmen, the first native Guatemalan convert, served as the first temple matron
During the 13-day open house, 24,206 people toured the temple and missionaries distributed approximately 3,700 copies of the Book of Mormon

name: Guayaquil Ecuador Temple
address: Calle Rosendo Maridueña 8845, Urdesa Norte, 090510 Guayaquil, Ecuador
facts: First temple built in Ecuador and the 58th operating temple of the Church
Experienced the longest delay between announcement and groundbreaking of any temple - over 14 years from March 1982 to August 1996
President Gordon B. Hinckley personally selected the site location, stating: 'I looked out over the city and had a feeling...as clear as anything that this was the site for the temple'
The temple features Brazilian Asa Branca granite exterior with a single attached end spire topped with an Angel Moroni statue
Built on a 6.2-acre site with 45,000 square feet of floor area, the temple includes 4 instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), 3 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Public open house ran for 24 days from June 23 to July 17, 1999, attracting an average of 1,800 daily visitors with peak attendance reaching 15,200 visitors
The temple complex includes an arrival center, patron housing, and an on-site distribution center, but no public visitors' center

name: Halifax Nova Scotia Temple
address: 44 Cumberland Dr, Dartmouth, Nova Scotia B2V 2C7, Canada
facts: The Halifax Nova Scotia Temple is the 64th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Built with white Bethel granite exterior finish and features a single attached spire topped with an Angel Moroni statue
The temple was dedicated on the same day as the Regina Saskatchewan Temple due to a mechanical plane problem that delayed President Gordon B. Hinckley's travel
Total floor area of 10,700 square feet (994 square meters) with a height of 71 feet (21.6 meters), situated on 2 acres at an elevation of 209 feet

Members traveled significant distances of 6-8 hours to attend the dedication, demonstrating their devotion and commitment to temple worship

name: Hamburg Germany Temple

facts: Will be the 3rd temple in Germany, joining Frankfurt Germany Temple and Freiberg Germany Temple

name: Hamilton New Zealand Temple

facts: First temple built in the Southern Hemisphere
Second temple built in Polynesia, following the Laie Hawaii Temple
Built entirely by volunteer missionary labor, making it a unique construction achievement
Features 45,251 square feet across three levels with 75 rooms including two instruction rooms and five sealing rooms
Stands 156 feet tall with a single attached end spire, constructed of reinforced concrete block and white painted structural steel
Located on a 35-acre site in Temple View, Hamilton
Contains unique murals in the Celestial Room
Recently renovated and rededicated in October 2022 after a four-year closure

name: Harare Zimbabwe Temple
address: 65 Enterprise Road, Highlands, Harare, Zimbabwe
facts: First temple in Zimbabwe and the 9th temple in Africa, serving approximately 26,000 Church members in 6 stakes and 2 districts

Exterior constructed with Corobrik Golden Wheat Travertine face brick and concrete, with design inspired by triangular forms found in Zimbabwe's architecture
Contains 45 art pieces focusing on Jesus Christ and the Creation, with art glass featuring geometric and floral motifs representing the flame lily, Zimbabwe's national flower

Located at 4,990 feet (1,521 meters) elevation on a 6.7-acre site with landscape featuring local trees including feather duster and jacaranda, replacing a 13-hour journey members previously made to the Johannesburg South Africa Temple

name: Harrisburg Pennsylvania Temple
address: 605 Rutherford Road, Harrisburg, Pennsylvania 17109, United States
facts: The temple will be built on a 5.36-acre (2.2 hectare) site at 605 Rutherford Road in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.
The temple will have approximately 20,000 square feet (1,858 square meters) with a single-story design featuring a single attached central tower.
This is the third temple announced for Pennsylvania, joining the Philadelphia Pennsylvania Temple (dedicated September 2016) and Pittsburgh Pennsylvania Temple.
The temple is located in the Susquehanna Valley in south-central Pennsylvania at an elevation of 493 feet (150 meters).

Pennsylvania has significant early Church history - the Book of Mormon was partially translated in Harmony, Pennsylvania, and the first Church members were baptized in the nearby Susquehanna River in May 1829.
The temple will serve over 52,000 Church members in more than 100 congregations across Pennsylvania.
The exterior rendering was released on December 16, 2024, showing a single-story classical design with a central spire.

name: Hartford Connecticut Temple

Exterior is finished with granite quarried in China and features a New England-style low stone fence
Stands 117 feet 2 inches (35.7 meters) tall with a single attached end spire topped by an angel Moroni statue
Encompasses 32,246 square feet (2,996 square meters) of floor space on an 11.3-acre site

The temple site was selected after investigating 20 different locations in the area

Henry B. Eyring's dedicatory prayer expressed the wish for the temple to be 'a house of sanctification and holiness' and extend its influence into families, homes, neighborhoods, and communities

facts: The Helena Montana Temple is the 178th dedicated temple and the second temple built in Montana

name: Helsinki Finland Temple
address: Leppäsillantie 3, FI-02620 Espoo, Finland
facts: The Helsinki Finland Temple was the 124th operating temple of the Church and the 10th temple built in Europe.
The temple is constructed with Olympia white granite from Italy for the main exterior, with brown Finnish granite used for retaining walls.
At 139 feet tall, the temple stands at an elevation of 87 feet and is located approximately 7 miles northwest of downtown Helsinki.
The temple covers 16,350 square feet and sits on a 7.4-acre site. It features 2 instruction rooms with two-stage progressive ordinances, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry.
This was the third temple built in the Nordic countries and is the second northernmost temple of the Church.
At the time of dedication, the temple district covered two continents and 12 time zones, serving members across a vast geographic area.
The public open house attracted 55,633 visitors over 16 days, with 5,711 comment cards submitted.
The Helsinki Finland Temple was the last temple dedicated by President Gordon B. Hinckley before his passing.

name: Hermosillo Sonora Mexico Temple
address: General Pedro Garcia Conde No 303, Esquina Con Juan José Ríos, Colonia Pitic, 83150 Hermosillo, Sonora, Mexico
facts: The temple was the 72nd operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the first temple built in the state of Sonora, Mexico.
The temple features white marble veneer exterior with a single attached spire topped by a statue of the angel Moroni.
The temple has a total floor area of 10,769 square feet (1,000 square meters) on a 1.54-acre site at an elevation of 758 feet (231 meters).
The temple contains two instruction rooms using a two-stage progressive presentation, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry.

The first LDS missionaries arrived in Mexico in 1875, with the first converts baptized near Hermosillo by Louis Garff and Meliton Trejo, making this temple particularly significant to the region's LDS history.
The temple site includes a distribution center but has no visitors' center or patron housing facilities.

Site area: 0.31 acres (0.1 hectares)

The temple stands 106 feet 4 inches tall with a total floor area of 34,000 square feet, featuring a single attached end tower topped with an Angel Moroni statue
Exterior is finished with Aero Cream limestone quarried from Myra, Turkey, and the design was inspired by Indianapolis memorials and monuments

The first known Church members in Brazil immigrated from Germany in 1913, and Brazil became the third country outside the United States with 50 stakes in 1988.

name: Johannesburg South Africa Temple

facts: First temple built in Africa and the first in South Africa, becoming the 36th operating temple of the Church

Constructed with masonry exterior and gray slate roof at an elevation of 5,765 feet (1,757 meters)
Nearly 19,000 visitors attended the public open house held for 11 days from July 30 to August 10, 1985
The temple's dedication enabled temple presence on every habitable continent for the first time in Church history

The first convert in South Africa was baptized in 1853, over 130 years before the temple was dedicated

name: Jordan River Utah Temple

facts: The Jordan River Utah Temple was the 20th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the 7th temple built in Utah
This was the second temple built in the Salt Lake Valley and the first of two temples located in the same city (along with Oquirrh Mountain Utah Temple)
The temple was entirely funded by donations from local members, and the 15-acre temple site was gifted to the Church
The temple stands 219 feet (66.8 meters) tall with a total floor area of 148,236 square feet, featuring 6 instruction rooms and 16 sealing rooms
This is the only temple dedicated by President Marion G. Romney and one of only 5 temples worldwide featuring an Angel Moroni statue holding gold plates
The exterior is finished with cast stone containing white marble chips, while the tower uses lightweight fiberglass (cemlite) to reduce structural weight
After a major renovation beginning in 2016, the temple was rededicated on May 20, 2018, by President Henry B. Eyring, with updates to mechanical systems, interior finishes, and landscaping

name: Juchitán de Zaragoza Mexico Temple

name: Kananga Democratic Republic of the Congo Temple

name: Kansas City Missouri Temple

facts: The Kansas City Missouri Temple is the second temple built in Missouri and the 137th temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints to be dedicated.
The temple site covers 8.05 acres (3.3 hectares) and has a total floor area of 32,000 square feet with a height of 150 feet (45.7 meters).
The temple features two attached end spires with an angel Moroni statue on top and is constructed with precast concrete for the exterior.
White oak harvested from Adam-ondi-Ahman, a historic LDS site in Daviess County, Missouri, was used in the temple's interior woodwork.
During the public open house from April 7-28, 2012, nearly 91,000 people toured the temple over 21 days.
The temple won the PCI Design Award for Best Religious Structure in 2012, recognizing its exceptional precast concrete design.
The temple contains two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive ordinance system), three sealing rooms, and one baptistry.

This temple represents the fulfillment of early attempts by Latter-day Saints to build temples in Missouri, with the first temple site dedicated in Independence in 1831 and another in Far West in 1838.

name: Kirtland Temple

timeline.by: Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery witnessed

timeline.milestone: Ownership Transferred to The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

facts: First temple erected in this dispensation, making it a pivotal historic site for early Latter-day Saint history

Built with sandstone stuccoed with plaster exterior and was guarded at night during construction to protect from mobs and vandals
On April 3, 1836, Moses, Elias, and Elijah appeared to Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery, restoring priesthood keys for the gathering of Israel, the gospel of Abraham, and sealing ordinances
The dedication ceremony on March 27, 1836 included extraordinary spiritual manifestations including visions, prophecies, the gift of tongues, and the appearance of angels
The School of the Prophets was established three years before temple dedication and met in a room above the Newel K. Whitney Store, serving as a forerunner to temple worship
After Saints largely abandoned Kirtland two years after dedication, the temple was leased as a public school and later used for private worship and public functions
Owned by the Community of Christ for 144 years before ownership was transferred back to The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints on March 5, 2024
Construction took nearly three years from groundbreaking in June 1833 to dedication in March 1836, with early Saints calling it the 'Lord's House'
Now operates as a historic Church property open for free public tours, reopened to visitors on March 25, 2024

The public open house from December 19, 1955 to February 18, 1956 attracted 682,361 visitors over 61 days.

The temple exterior is faced with Sierra white granite and features two 35-foot relief sculptures depicting Christ teaching, symbolizing the temple's mission and the diversity of Bay Area residents.

The temple has a total floor area of 80,157 square feet and contains four instruction rooms, seven sealing rooms, and one baptistry, making it the first temple with more than one instruction room connected to a central celestial room.
Elder George Albert Smith had a vision in 1928 of 'a white temple of the Lord high upon those hills' nearly three decades before the temple was announced in 1961.
The temple underwent major renovations in 1990 and again from 2018-2019, during which windows were added to the exterior. It was rededicated by Elder Dallin H. Oaks on June 16, 2019.
The temple grounds feature cascading waterfalls and beautifully maintained gardens, along with a visitors' center that houses a Christus statue.

name: Oaxaca Mexico Temple

facts: The temple was the 74th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the 7th temple built in Mexico
The exterior is finished with beautiful white marble quarried from Torreón, Mexico, giving it a distinctive and elegant appearance
Located at an elevation of 5,040 feet (1,536 meters), the temple sits in the mountainous region of southern Mexico
The temple features a compact design with 10,700 square feet of floor space on a 1.87-acre site, including two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry

At the site dedication ceremony, Carl B. Pratt referenced Brigham Young's prophecy about hundreds of temples being built, emphasizing the temple's role in fulfilling that vision and its potential for missionary work in the region
The temple includes a distribution center on-site but does not have a visitors' center or patron housing facilities

facts: The 14th temple of the Church, and the first temple dedicated in the state of Utah (as opposed to Utah Territory)
Originally designed without an angel Moroni statue on the spire; the statue was added 30 years later in 2002
Features 6 instruction rooms, 9 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry across 112,232 square feet of floor space
The exterior is made of granite that was quarried and fabricated in China
At the original dedication in 1972, President Harold B. Lee finished the dedicatory prayer when President Joseph Fielding Smith became too weak to continue

Groundbreaking occurred on President David O. McKay's 96th birthday
One of only four temples with six instruction rooms

name: Okinawa Japan Temple
address: 7-11-32 Matsumoto, Okinawa-shi, Okinawa-ken, Okinawa 904-2151, Japan
facts: The Okinawa Japan Temple is the 186th operating temple worldwide and the 4th temple in Japan, located in a region famous for having some of the longest living people in the world.
The temple's art glass features bingata, a traditional Okinawan stencil art style with a three-layered painted design that looks different from inside and outside the building.

Architectural elements pay homage to traditional Okinawan structures, including segmented arches inspired by stone walls at historical castles, decorative floral bands referencing traditional Japanese gateways, and an origami-inspired staircase design.

The temple sits on just 0.55 acres (0.2 hectares) with a total floor area of 12,437 square feet (1,155 square meters), stands 105 feet tall, and is constructed with Sunset Gold Chinese granite with a single attached end spire.

The temple symbolizes peace on an island with a tragic World War II history and represents unity between American military members and local Japanese Church members. The first Latter-day Saint servicemen's conference was held in Okinawa on July 8, 1945, and Elder Joseph Fielding Smith formally dedicated Okinawa for gospel preaching on August 14, 1955.
The temple grounds feature tropical landscaping with native plants including fountain palms and Chinese hibiscus, plus a Japanese rock garden that complements the temple's cultural design elements.

name: Oquirrh Mountain Utah Temple
address: 11022 S 4000 W, South Jordan, Utah 84009-5797
facts: The Oquirrh Mountain Utah Temple was the first temple built in the same city as another operating temple (Jordan River Utah Temple), both located in South Jordan.
The temple's exterior features Uinta Gold Granite from China and stands 183 feet tall with a single attached end spire topped by an Angel Moroni statue.

The temple spans 60,000 square feet and includes 4 instruction rooms, 7 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry. Interior materials include limestone from Morocco and Egypt, and rift-cut White Oak from Indiana and Kentucky.
The Celestial Room features a stunning Schonebek chandelier with Swarovski crystals that stands 15 feet tall and contains 19,447 individual crystals.
Lightning struck the Angel Moroni statue on June 13, 2009, during the public open house period, but the statue survived the strike.
The dedication on August 21-23, 2009, occurred on President Thomas S. Monson's 82nd birthday and marked the first time church services were cancelled statewide in Utah for a temple dedication.
The temple is the 130th dedicated temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and sits at an elevation of 4,783 feet on an 11-acre site.
Interior murals were painted by 'art missionaries' and the temple features Schonebek chandeliers throughout with genuine Swarovski crystals.

facts: The Quetzaltenango Guatemala Temple is the second temple built in Guatemala and the 136th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

The temple sits on 6.47 acres and has a total floor area of 21,085 square feet with a single attached tower rising 80 feet high.
Features exterior walls made from precast concrete panels imported from Mexico, topped with an Angel Moroni statue installed on October 1, 2010.
The public open house attracted 126,000 visitors over 15 days (November 11-26, 2011), demonstrating strong community interest.
This was the first temple dedicated by President Dieter F. Uchtdorf, with the dedication ceremony broadcast to meetinghouses throughout Guatemala.

The temple uses the same core architectural plan as the Snowflake Arizona and Winter Quarters Nebraska Temples, featuring a two-stage progressive design with two instruction rooms and two sealing rooms.

Located in Apex, North Carolina, the temple serves members throughout the region with a phone contact at (+1) 919-362-4135

name: Red Cliffs Utah Temple
address: 1555 S Red Cliffs Temple Ln, St. George, Utah 84790, United States
facts: The Red Cliffs Utah Temple is the 20th temple built in Utah and the 2nd temple in Washington County, following the historic St. George Utah Temple from 1877.
The temple features a total floor area of 99,055 square feet (9,203 square meters), stands 230 feet (70.1 meters) tall, and sits at an elevation of 2,618 feet (798 meters) on a 15.31-acre site.
The temple's design is inspired by Southern Utah's desert landscape, featuring precast concrete exterior panels with a coral-beige tone and an intricate cottonwood leaf motif throughout.

Art glass throughout the temple features local flora including succulents, bearclaw poppies, and Indian paintbrush, with a color scheme of coral, purple, green, and gold reflecting the desert environment.

The temple includes four instruction rooms, six sealing rooms, one baptistry, a single attached central spire topped with an angel Moroni statue, and reflecting pools integrated into the landscaping design.
As the 189th dedicated temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, it serves the growing membership in the St. George area and surrounding communities.

Features light gray granite exterior with a single attached spire rising 130 feet (39.6 meters) tall, crowned by a 13½-foot Angel Moroni statue placed on March 13, 2003

The temple has 17,300 square feet (1,607 square meters) of total floor area on a 4.6-acre (1.9-hectare) site at 1,722 feet (525 meters) elevation, with two instruction rooms, three sealing rooms, and one baptistry

The temple features a light gray granite exterior with a single attached spire topped by an Angel Moroni statue
Construction crews worked around the clock to complete landscaping and exterior finishing before the dedication ceremony, with members traveling hundreds of miles to attend
The temple sits at an elevation of 1,889 feet (576 meters) on a 1-acre site and contains 10,700 square feet of floor space with two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry
Saskatchewan province has approximately 1 million residents with about 4,500 members of the Church, making this temple a significant regional center for worship

The temple is located in San Sebastián, Retalhuleu, a region that has been part of the Church's presence in Guatemala since the first missionaries arrived in 1947.

facts: The Salt Lake Temple is the largest temple by square footage in the Church, with a total floor area of 382,207 square feet.
Construction of the temple took 40 years, beginning with the groundbreaking on February 14, 1853, and culminating in its dedication on April 6-24, 1893.
The temple walls are constructed of quartz monzonite stone from Little Cottonwood Canyon, with walls 9 feet thick at the base and 6 feet thick at the top.
The temple stands 222 feet tall with 6 attached spires, topped by a 14-foot-tall Angel Moroni statue, and sits at an elevation of 4,335 feet on a 10-acre site.
The temple features 5 instruction rooms, 22 sealing rooms, and 2 baptistries, and was the 4th temple dedicated in Utah Territory.

The temple features Portuguese colonial-style architecture with a single attached central tower standing 148 feet 8 inches (45.3 meters) tall at an elevation of 82 feet (25 meters).
The temple has a total floor area of 29,963 square feet (2,784 square meters) and includes two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry.
Architectural features include art glass windows featuring the red mandacarú flower (a native Brazilian cactus) and decorative stone sourced from Brazil.

name: San Antonio Texas Temple
address: 20080 Stone Oak Pkwy, San Antonio, Texas 78258-6920, United States
facts: The San Antonio Texas Temple was the 120th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the 4th temple built in Texas.
The temple features a single attached spire topped with a 13-foot gold-leafed Angel Moroni statue, which was placed on September 21, 2004.
Built on a 5.5-acre site at an elevation of 1,225 feet, the temple has a total floor area of 16,800 square feet and is finished with granite exterior.
The temple contains 2 instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry, serving members throughout South Texas.
A cultural celebration was held at the Alamodome before the dedication, with approximately 20,000 attendees participating in the festivities.
The groundbreaking ceremony on March 29, 2003, which also served as the site dedication, coincided with the groundbreaking of the Helsinki Finland Temple.
During the 21-day public open house from April 16 to May 7, 2005, thousands of visitors toured the temple before it was dedicated by President Gordon B. Hinckley on May 22, 2005.

name: San Diego California Temple
address: 7474 Charmant Dr, San Diego, California 92122-5000, United States
facts: The San Diego California Temple was the 45th operating temple of the Church and the third built in California, featuring two main towers with four smaller spires surrounding each tower.
The temple sits on 7.2 acres and has a total floor area of 72,000 square feet, standing 169 feet tall with an Angel Moroni statue atop the eastern spire.
The groundbreaking ceremony on February 27, 1988, was significant as it was President Ezra Taft Benson's first temple groundbreaking after recovering from a mild heart attack.
Over 720,000 visitors attended the original open house in 1993, and the temple was dedicated in 23 sessions with 49,273 attendees by Gordon B. Hinckley.
The temple features a unique star-shaped atrium with a colorful garden and a magnificent two-story Celestial Room with art glass and a grand staircase connecting both levels.
The temple's architects, William S. Lewis Jr., Dennis Hyndman, and Shelly Hyndman, were Roman Catholic and had never toured an LDS temple interior before visiting the Las Vegas temple's open house.
The temple received recognition as Headliner of the Year in 1993 by the San Diego Press Club and was awarded the Silver Anvil Award for outstanding public relations efforts.
The temple closed for renovation on July 31, 2023, with a planned rededication ceremony on August 23, 2026, following an extensive open house period.

name: San José Costa Rica Temple

facts: The San José Costa Rica Temple was the 87th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and the second temple built in Central America.

With a total floor area of 10,700 square feet, the temple includes two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry.
The temple serves approximately 32,000 members across 5 stakes and 17 districts in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Panama.
Located at an elevation of 3,108 feet (947 meters) in Belén, Heredia, the temple sits on a 1.93-acre site and stands 71 feet tall.

Built on a 2.61-acre site originally intended for a Church school, with a total floor area of 20,831 square feet
The temple has a single attached end spire rising 76 feet high with an Angel Moroni statue, and sits at an elevation of 1,950 feet
Served 140,000 Chilean members at its first dedication in 1983 and over 535,000 members at its 2006 rededication
During the first open house in 1983, approximately 55 busloads of visitors toured the temple, drawing extensive media coverage
The 2006 rededication was preceded by a cultural celebration featuring 4,000 youth, and 62,065 people toured during the open house

name: Santiago Dominican Republic Temple
address: Site location not yet announced
facts: Will be the second temple in the Dominican Republic, following the Santo Domingo temple dedicated in 2000
Located in Santiago, the second largest city in the Dominican Republic
Announced during the 194th Semiannual General Conference by President Russell M. Nelson
The Dominican Republic has nearly 150,000 Latter-day Saints in approximately 200 congregations
Currently in planning and approval phase with site location not yet determined

name: Santiago Philippines Temple
address: East of Camella Isabella Main Road, Barangay Malvar, City of Santiago, Isabela, Philippines
facts: The Santiago Philippines Temple will be the 10th temple announced for the Philippines, serving members in the northern Luzon region.
The temple site comprises 14.47 acres (5.9 hectares) at an elevation of 260 feet (79 meters) above sea level.
The temple will have a total floor area of 18,850 square feet (1,751 square meters) and feature a single attached end tower design.
Located approximately 225 miles northeast of Manila in the City of Santiago, Isabela, the temple will serve members in a region previously requiring long travel distances to existing temples.
The temple complex will include not only the temple itself but also a patron housing facility, distribution center, and meetinghouse to serve the local Latter-day Saint community.

name: Santiago West Chile Temple
address: Primo de Rivera 1551, Comuna de Maipu, Santiago, Chile
facts: The Santiago West Chile Temple is the fourth temple built in Chile, following the Santiago Chile Temple (1983), the Concepción Chile Temple (2018), and the Antofagasta Chile Temple (2024).
This is the seventh temple to be built in a city that already has an existing temple, allowing greater access to temple ordinances for members in the Santiago area.
The temple features a single attached central tower design, which is a distinctive architectural element visible from the surrounding community.
The temple has a total floor area of 12,500 square feet (1,161 square meters) and includes one instruction room, one sealing room, and one baptistry.
Located at an elevation of 1,582 feet (482 meters) in the Comuna de Maipu, the temple was announced during the 38th anniversary year of the Santiago Chile Temple's dedication.
Elder Alan R. Walker, who presided at the groundbreaking ceremony, stated: 'This temple will stand as a beacon of light, a place of peace and a refuge from the world.'

name: Santo Domingo Dominican Republic Temple
location: Santo Domingo, Distrito Nacional, Dominican Republic
address: Avenida Bolivar N° 825, Los Robles, 10106 Santo Domingo, Distrito Nacional, Dominican Republic
facts: First temple built in the Caribbean and the Dominican Republic, serving as the 99th operating temple of the Church
Features Regina white granite exterior with a single attached end spire crowned by an Angel Moroni statue
Built on a 6.42-acre site offering views of the Caribbean Sea, located near the National Music Conservatory in Santo Domingo
Contains 67,000 square feet of floor space with 4 instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), 4 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry, with a total elevation of 132 feet
Site dedication and groundbreaking both occurred on the same day (August 18, 1996) by Elder Richard G. Scott, with the temple dedicated by President Gordon B. Hinckley on September 17, 2000

name: Santos Brazil Temple
address: Avenida Doutor Waldemar Leão, 305, Jabaquara, Santos–SP, Brazil
facts: Will be the 16th temple constructed in Brazil, serving a country with 1.5 million Latter-day Saints in over 2,100 congregations
Two-story building with a total floor area of 23,000 square feet (2,137 square meters)
Will be constructed on the site of the existing Jabaquara Ward meetinghouse in Santos
Located in Santos, a southeastern port city in São Paulo state, at an elevation of 11 feet (4 meters)
The groundbreaking ceremony is scheduled for August 1, 2026, to be presided over by Elder Ronald M. Barcellos of the Brazil Area Presidency

name: São Paulo Brazil Temple
address: Av. Prof. Francisco Morato, 2390, Caxingui, 05512-300 São Paulo-SP, Brazil
facts: First temple built in South America and the 17th temple dedicated by the Church
Site covers 1.85 acres with 59,246 square feet of floor area and stands 101 feet tall
Constructed with reinforced concrete faced with cast stone made of quartz and marble aggregates
Gold-leafed angel Moroni statue was added to the spire on August 20, 2003, 25 years after the original dedication
At the time of dedication in 1978, there were only 15 stakes and 54,000 members in Brazil; by the 2004 rededication, membership had grown to over 867,000
Features two instruction rooms, four sealing rooms, and one baptistry
First South American missionary training center opened at this location in 1979
First temple visitors' center in South America opened on January 20, 2019
During the 2004 rededication open house, 99,000 people toured the temple, generating 6,000 missionary visit requests
Located next to a major shopping mall and Metro station, making it easily accessible to members
Temple sits at an elevation of 2,416 feet (736 meters)
The dedication sessions ran from October 30 to November 2, 1978, with multiple sessions conducted by President Spencer W. Kimball

name: São Paulo East Brazil Temple
address: Avenida Guilherme Giorgi, 1091, Vila Carrão, 03422-001 São Paulo–SP, Brazil
facts: Will be the 12th temple built in Brazil and the 3rd temple in São Paulo state

Located on a 10.7-acre (4.3-hectare) site at elevation 2,564 feet (782 meters) in Vila Carrão neighborhood
Planned total floor area of 46,050 square feet (4,278 square meters)

Brazil operates 35 missions, second only to the United States, and had 277 stakes as of 2020
Currently in planning and approval phase with no groundbreaking date announced; architectural renderings have not yet been publicly released

name: Sapporo Japan Temple
address: 1-6-1 Oyachi-Nishi, Atsubetsu-ku, Sapporo, Hokkaido 004-0042, Japan
facts: The Sapporo Japan Temple is the 151st dedicated temple of the Church and the 3rd temple built in Japan, following Tokyo (1980) and Fukuoka (2000).
The temple features exterior granite quarried and carved in China with a distinctive sea-wave motif, while the interior showcases marble flooring from Israel and Egypt.
Interior woodwork includes stained cherry wood and Japanese white birch, with polished bronze door hardware made in Japan, reflecting local craftsmanship.

The temple sits on 9.8 acres and includes a complex of buildings with mission facilities, a distribution center, patron housing, and an arrival center. The landscape features traditional Japanese elements including distinctive trees, stones, a pond, and waterfall.

With a total floor area of 48,480 square feet and standing 127 feet tall, the temple includes 2 instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), 3 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry. It features a central spire with four lanterned spires and an Angel Moroni statue.
Missionaries first arrived in Sapporo in 1905, making it one of the early missionary areas in Japan. The temple serves members across Hokkaido, the northernmost island of Japan.

name: Saratoga Springs Utah Temple
address: 987 South Ensign Drive, Saratoga Springs, Utah 84045-3839, United States
facts: The 179th operating temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, with a total floor area of 97,836 square feet (9,089 square meters) on a 22.71-acre site
Features a single attached central spire crowned with an Angel Moroni statue, standing 199 feet 9 inches tall with beige precast concrete panels exterior

Landscaping features native plant species, a tiered water feature in the entrance plaza, manicured gardens, and grassy lawns designed to complement the natural desert environment

name: Savai'i Samoa Temple
address: Lot 1098 to Lot 1105, Fataloa, Salelologa, Savai'i, Samoa
facts: The Savai'i Samoa Temple will be the second temple in Samoa, joining the Apia Samoa Temple which was dedicated in 1983.
The temple will feature a single attached central spire and span 29,630 square feet (2,753 square meters) on a 4.6-acre site.
More than 85,000 Latter-day Saints live in Samoa, representing a significant portion of the population.
The temple will include two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, one baptistry, plus patron housing, an ancillary building, a distribution center, and a temple president's residence.
The temple site is located near Don Bosco College and Vocational Technical Center in Salelologa, the main ferry terminal town for Savai'i island.
The temple was announced during a historic visit by Samoa's Prime Minister Fiame Naomi Mata'afa to Salt Lake City.
The temple elevation will be 88 feet (27 meters) tall.

name: Seattle Washington Temple

One of only 5 temples with an Angel Moroni statue holding gold plates
Total floor area of 110,000 square feet (10,219 square meters) with 4 instruction rooms, 13 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Features a single attached end spire rising 179 feet (54.6 meters) high

Originally had a red strobe warning light near the Angel Moroni statue due to proximity to a nearby airfield, which was removed in 1983
Located at an elevation of 412 feet (126 meters) in Bellevue, near Interstate 90
During the dedication, women protesting the Church's stance on the Equal Rights Amendment chained themselves to the temple gates

name: Seoul Korea Temple

facts: First temple built in mainland Asia and the first in South Korea, making it the 37th temple of the Church in operation worldwide

Total floor area of 28,057 square feet (2,607 square meters) on a 1-acre site, with facilities including 4 instruction rooms, 3 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Located near three major universities: Ewha Womans University, Yonsei University, and Sogang University in Seoul

name: Shanghai People's Republic of China Temple

In an initial phase of facility use, entry will be by appointment only

For more than two decades, temple-worthy members in the People's Republic of China have attended the Hong Kong China Temple
Site location remains unknown as of the announcement phase
Still in planning and approval phase with local authorities

name: Singapore Temple

Missionary work in Singapore began in 1968

name: Smithfield Utah Temple
address: N 800 West and W 100 North, Smithfield, Utah
facts: The temple features two attached end towers and a three-story design with a total floor area of 81,000 square feet (7,525 square meters)
Located on a 13.3-acre site at an elevation of 4,506 feet (1,373 meters), the property was previously farmed by the LaMont Poulsen family for over 160 years
The temple contains four instruction rooms, four sealing rooms, and two baptistries to serve the Cache Valley community
This is the second temple built in Cache Valley, joining the historic Logan Utah Temple, and the 26th temple constructed in Utah

Elder Quentin L. Cook, who presided at both the site dedication and groundbreaking ceremony on June 18, 2022, remarked: 'I love this beautiful valley... It is among the most beautiful places on earth.'

name: Snowflake Arizona Temple
address: 1875 W Canyon Dr, Snowflake, Arizona 85937-6014, United States
facts: Second temple built in Arizona, following the Mesa Arizona Temple as the 108th operating temple of the Church
Located on 7.5 acres on 'Temple Hill' next to a golf course, with the site requiring removal of approximately 8 feet from the top of a knoll
Features 18,621 square feet of floor space and stands 60 feet tall at an elevation of 5,746 feet above sea level
Exterior constructed with two-tone imported polished granite from China, with a single attached spire topped by an angel Moroni statue
Sister building to the Winter Quarters Nebraska Temple, sharing similar architectural design and core plan with the Quetzaltenango Guatemala Temple
Interior design reflects local pioneer and Native American history, with custom furniture featuring symbolic pioneer and Native American designs
Contains two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry for temple ordinances
The town of Snowflake was named after early settler William J. Flake and Apostle Erastus Snow, reflecting the area's rich LDS pioneer heritage
Features art glass windows with beveled cuts, stained-glass windows depicting Christ with children and adults, and dark cherry and light painted wood throughout
In March 2017, the angel Moroni statue was repositioned from facing east to facing west, and features a rustic water feature on the lower parking level

name: Spanish Fork Utah Temple
address: 100 South and 2550 East, Spanish Fork, Utah
facts: Ninth temple announced for Utah County and the 32nd temple in the state of Utah
Located on an 8.7-acre (3.5-hectare) site at an elevation of 4,729 feet (1,442 meters)

Located in southern Utah Valley near Utah Lake and the Wasatch Range
Site preparation includes soil core sampling and groundwater measurement, with design work currently in progress

name: Spokane Washington Temple
address: 13710 E 40th Ave, Spokane, Washington 99206, United States
facts: The Spokane Washington Temple is the second temple built in Washington state and the 59th temple in operation
The temple's groundbreaking ceremony was held on the same day as the Detroit Michigan Temple's groundbreaking on October 10, 1998
The temple features a single attached spire with an angel Moroni statue and is finished with granite exterior
The temple sits on 2 acres (0.8 hectares) with a total floor area of 10,700 square feet (994 square meters)
The temple is located at an elevation of 2,023 feet (617 meters) above sea level
The temple contains two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry for sacred ordinances
In March 2009, the temple received a new angel Moroni statue that faces west, replacing the previous statue
The temple held an 8-day public open house from August 6-14, 1999, before its dedication
The temple was dedicated by President Gordon B. Hinckley over three days from August 21-23, 1999
A touching story involves a truck driver who unknowingly transported sacred temple artifacts and became emotional upon learning their significance

name: Springfield Missouri Temple
address: 2720 East Farm Road #188, Springfield, Missouri
facts: Third temple to be built in Missouri, following the Kansas City Missouri Temple (2012) and St. Louis Missouri Temple (1997)
Features a single-story design with a single attached central spire on a 38-acre site at an elevation of 1,275 feet
Total floor area of 29,000 square feet with 2 instruction rooms, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Serves over 75,000 Latter-day Saints in 160 congregations across Missouri
Missouri holds historical significance as Independence was an important early gathering place for the Church in the 1830s

name: St. George Utah Temple
address: 250 E 400 S, St. George, Utah 84770-3699, United States
facts: First temple built in Utah Territory and the oldest continuously operating temple in the Church
First temple where endowments for the dead were performed
Only temple completed during Brigham Young's presidency
Built on swampy ground that was packed with volcanic rock; foundation prepared using a cannon as a pile driver
Originally featured 18 sealing rooms, more than any other temple until the Salt Lake Temple renovation
Exterior constructed of native red sandstone, plastered white, with a single attached end tower standing 175 feet tall
Total floor area of 143,969 square feet on a 6.5-acre site
Originally used live acting for endowment presentations
The Founding Fathers of the United States reportedly appeared twice to Wilford Woodruff in this temple, requesting their temple work be performed
Features three instruction rooms, seventeen sealing rooms, and one baptistry

name: St. Louis Missouri Temple
location: Town and Country, Missouri, United States
address: 12555 N Outer Forty Dr, St. Louis, Missouri 63141-8620

timeline.by: Gordon B. Hinckley (first two days), James E. Faust (next 1.5 days), Thomas S. Monson (final 1.5 days)

facts: The St. Louis Missouri Temple was the first temple built in Missouri, holding special significance as the state where early Church members faced severe persecution in the 1830s.
Each member of the First Presidency independently selected the same temple site location without prior consultation, which was seen as a remarkable confirmation.
The temple features a single attached end tower rising 150 feet high with the Angel Moroni statue, constructed with cast stone and Bethel white granite with thermal finish.
Nearly 5,000 people attended the groundbreaking ceremony on October 30, 1993, and approximately 260,000 visitors toured the temple during the 28-day public open house.

The temple dedication spanned five days (June 1-5, 1997) with different members of the First Presidency presiding: Gordon B. Hinckley presided over the first two days, James E. Faust the next 1.5 days, and Thomas S. Monson the final 1.5 days.
Located on a 14-acre site near Interstate 270 in Town and Country, the temple has a total floor area of 58,749 square feet and features 4 instruction rooms, 4 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry.

name: St. Paul Minnesota Temple
address: 2150 Hadley Ave N, Oakdale, Minnesota 55128-4505, United States
facts: First temple built in Minnesota, the 69th dedicated temple of the Church
Features light gray granite veneer exterior with a single attached spire topped by an angel Moroni statue
Total floor area of 11,411 square feet with a height of 71 feet and elevation of 1,043 feet
Contains two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry
Built on a 7.5-acre site in Oakdale, serving members who previously had to travel 7-14 hours to the nearest temple
Located in the Chicago Illinois Temple District, with the first stake in Minnesota organized in 1960

name: Star Valley Wyoming Temple
address: 885 S Washington St, Afton, Wyoming 83110, United States
facts: First temple built in Wyoming, making it the 154th dedicated temple of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
The temple site was prophesied by Elder Moses Thatcher in the late 1870s, who named the valley 'Star Valley' and predicted a future temple would be built there
Located at an elevation of 6,218 feet (1,896 meters), with a height of 123 feet (37.5 meters) and a single attached end spire topped with an Angel Moroni statue
Features exterior glass with local fireweed flower design and a stained-glass depiction of Jesus Christ in the foyer, constructed with precast concrete panels
Built on a 43.6-acre site with a total floor area of 18,609 square feet, containing one instruction room, one sealing room, and one baptistry

name: Stockholm Sweden Temple
address: Tempelvägen 5, SE-13742 Västerhaninge, Sweden
facts: The Stockholm Sweden Temple was the 4th temple built in Europe and the 1st temple built in the Nordic countries.
The temple was designed by Swedish architect John Sjöström to harmonize with local Scandinavian architecture and features six detached spires with an Angel Moroni statue.
Construction was delayed over a year due to the discovery of Viking graves on the temple site. During groundbreaking, a torch was used to cut through the ice so that the earth could be turned.
During the 12-day public open house (June 10-22, 1985), 47,609 visitors toured the temple and 2,200 copies of the Book of Mormon were distributed.

The temple dedicatory sessions were translated into 4 languages: Swedish, Finnish, Norwegian, and Danish. Gordon B. Hinckley said at the first dedicatory session: 'This is the most significant day in the history of the Church in Scandinavia.'
The temple is located on 4.47 acres (1.8 hectares) at an elevation of 138 feet (42 meters) in Västerhaninge, Sweden.

The temple closed for renovation on March 25, 2023, for an expected 3-year renovation that will nearly double its size from 16,366 to approximately 31,000 square feet and will feature two instruction rooms with 40 seats each.
The temple district eventually expanded to include the Baltic States and Russia, serving Latter-day Saints across a vast geographic region.

name: Summit New Jersey Temple

timeline.by: President Russell M. Nelson at 194th Semiannual General Conference

facts: First temple in New Jersey
Approximately 35,000 Latter-day Saints in over 60 congregations in the state
Latter-day Saint missionaries first preached in New Jersey in 1832
Closest existing temples are Manhattan New York Temple and Philadelphia Pennsylvania Temple
Temple is in planning and approval phase with site location unknown

name: Sunnyvale California Temple
address: 771 West Fremont Avenue, Sunnyvale, California, United States

timeline.milestone: Renamed from San Jose California Temple

facts: The temple will have a total floor area of 30,000 square feet (2,787 square meters)
The site covers 4.73 acres (1.9 hectares) at an elevation of 181 feet (55 meters)
Features a single attached central tower architectural design
Will include 2 instruction rooms, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry
Located in Silicon Valley, one of the world's leading technology and innovation hubs
This is the 11th temple announced for California
Will include an accompanying distribution center on the same site
Originally announced as the San Jose California Temple, it was renamed to Sunnyvale California Temple on December 15, 2025

name: Suva Fiji Temple
address: LOT 2-20 Lakeba St, Samabula, Suva, Fiji
facts: First temple built in Melanesia and the 91st temple dedicated by the Church
Exterior features snow white granite imported from Italy with a single attached spire topped by a statue of the Angel Moroni
Design inspired by Fijian flowers, beaches, and jungles, with art glass by Tom Holdman Studios featuring local flora

Temple was renovated from 2014-2016; during the rededication on February 21, 2016, Tropical Cyclone Winston (one of the strongest cyclones in Southern Hemisphere history) was diverted from Suva just before landfall
The temple sits on 4.7 acres at an elevation of 308 feet, with a total floor area of 12,755 square feet including 2 instruction rooms, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry

name: Sydney Australia Temple
address: 756 Pennant Hills Road, Corner Pennant Hills Rd & Moseley St., Carlingford, New South Wales 2118, Australia
facts: First temple built in Australia, becoming the 28th dedicated temple of the Church
Located on 3 acres (1.2 hectares) in suburban Carlingford, about 12 miles northwest of downtown Sydney
Built with precast panels featuring a white quartz finish and terra cotta roof tiles, with a single attached end spire topped by an angel Moroni statue
Total floor area of 30,067 square feet (2,793 square meters) with two instruction rooms, three sealing rooms, and one baptistry
Temple site was previously used as a boys' school before being acquired for the temple
Bruce R. McConkie, who presided over the groundbreaking ceremony, was a former mission president of Australia and expressed hope for temples in Adelaide, Melbourne, Brisbane, and Perth
During the 12-day public open house in September 1984, thousands of Australians toured the temple, generating hundreds of information requests
The temple was rededicated in 1991 by Russell M. Nelson following an addition to the building
Located at an elevation of 418 feet (127 meters) above sea level

name: Syracuse Utah Temple
address: 1098 South 2500 West, Syracuse, Utah 84075

timeline.by: Elder Kevin R. Duncan, Quorum of the Seventy

facts: 24th temple built in Utah and the 3rd temple in Davis County
Features white Moncini granite exterior with a single attached end spire standing 219 feet 8 inches tall
Total floor area of 90,526 square feet with 4 instruction rooms, 4 sealing rooms, and 2 baptistries
Design motifs represent local flora around Great Salt Lake and wetlands, including snowball sand-verbena and desert paintbrush
Art glass reflects lake colors with blues, greens, and yellows representing 'Gateway to Antelope Island'
Located on 12.268 acres between Great Salt Lake and Interstate 15 at an elevation of 4,275 feet
Will serve members of 63 stakes in the region, easing demand on the Ogden Utah Temple
206th temple dedicated by The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

name: Tacloban City Philippines Temple
address: Tacloban Bypass Road, Brgy. Utap-Caibaan, Tacloban City, Leyte, Philippines
facts: Site size of 6.99 acres (2.8 hectares) with an elevation of 26 feet (8 meters)
Total floor area of 21,407 square feet (1,989 square meters) in a two-story design
Features a single attached end spire architectural design
Will include an arrival center, patron housing, and distribution center
Located in an area previously devastated by a deadly typhoon in 2013, symbolizing community resilience and recovery
Will be the 8th temple in the Philippines, serving a region with over 805,000 Latter-day Saints
Located in the Eastern Visayas region on Tacloban Bypass Road in Brgy. Utap-Caibaan

name: Tacoma Washington Temple
address: 1405 South 364th Way, Federal Way, Washington
facts: The Tacoma Washington Temple will be the 5th temple in Washington state and will serve over 280,000 Latter-day Saints in nearly 525 congregations throughout the region
The temple will feature a single attached end spire rising 186 feet (56.7 meters) tall with an exterior finish of granite
The 38,000 square foot temple will house four instruction rooms, three sealing rooms, and one baptistry, with total building size approximately 45,000 square feet including a utility building
The temple site encompasses 11.6 acres (4.7 hectares) in Federal Way, Washington, at an elevation of 200 feet (61 meters)
Missionaries from The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints first arrived in Washington in 1854, establishing a long history of Church presence in the Pacific Northwest

name: Taipei Taiwan Temple
address: 256 Ai Kuo East Road, Taipei 10642, Taiwan
facts: Third temple built in Asia and the first temple in Taiwan
Built on a site where a prison once stood, as noted by President Gordon B. Hinckley at the dedication

The public open house attracted over 20,000 visitors during its 11-day period from October 30 to November 10, 1984
At dedication, only 3 stakes existed on the island; 25 years later this grew to 10 stakes and 2 districts
The temple was nominated as one of the most beautiful buildings in Taipei
Contains 4 instruction rooms, 3 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry within its 9,945 square feet of floor space

name: Tallahassee Florida Temple

timeline.by: James B. Martino (North America Southeast Area President)

facts: Third temple in Florida, serving over 150,000 Church members across 33 stakes in the state
Members previously traveled 260 miles to the Orlando Florida Temple before this temple was built

Temple grounds feature native Florida landscaping including live oak, magnolias, crape myrtles, and eight varieties of azaleas as the signature landscape element
Located in the northeast section of Florida's capital city at an elevation of 204 feet (62 meters)

name: Tampa Florida Temple
address: 9445 Camden Fields Parkway, Riverview, Florida, United States
facts: The Tampa Florida Temple is the 4th temple built in Florida, serving over 160,000 Latter-day Saints across approximately 260 congregations in the state.
The temple site encompasses 12 acres (4.9 hectares) at an elevation of 24 feet (7 meters) in Riverview, Florida.
The temple will have a total floor area of 29,000 square feet (2,694 square meters) with a single attached central tower.
The temple will feature 2 instruction rooms, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry to serve the growing membership in the Tampa Bay area.
The original temple site was located in Valrico, Florida, but was changed to the current location in Riverview in April 2024.

At the site dedication ceremony on August 23, 2025, Elder Neil L. Andersen offered a dedicatory prayer asking that the temple 'might be a light and strength to the Latter-day Saints and to all the community.'
The groundbreaking ceremony and site dedication both occurred on the same day: August 23, 2025, with foundation work currently underway.
The temple address is 9445 Camden Fields Parkway in Riverview, providing convenient access for members throughout the Tampa Bay metropolitan area.

name: Tampico Mexico Temple
address: Av. Ejercito Mexicano #74 Con 3A Av., Colonia Loma del Gallo, 89480 Ciudad Madero, Tamaulipas, Mexico
facts: The Tampico Mexico Temple was the 83rd operating temple of the Church and the 7th in Mexico.
The temple exterior features Blanco Guardiano white marble quarried from Torreón, Mexico, giving it a distinctive regional character.
Built on a 2.96-acre site, the temple has a total floor area of 10,700 square feet and stands 71 feet tall with a single attached spire topped by an angel Moroni statue.

The temple was built to serve approximately 18,000 members across 4 stakes, 3 districts, and the Mexico Tampico Mission, reducing the need for members to travel 500 miles over the Sierra Madres mountain range to the Mexico City Temple.
The temple features a two-stage progressive ordinance room configuration with 2 instruction rooms, 2 sealing rooms, and 1 baptistry.
Located at an elevation of 73 feet in Ciudad Madero, the temple serves the Gulf Coast region of Mexico in the state of Tamaulipas.

name: Tarawa Kiribati Temple
address: Across from Kiribati House of Parliament, Ambo, South Tarawa, Kiribati

Exterior art glass features stylized local wildflowers, with a color palette of deep burgundy, lavender, and gold throughout the temple.
The temple was built on a 7.5-acre site at an elevation of 4,335 feet (1,322 meters), on the location of a former Church meetinghouse, and is the 5th temple built in the Salt Lake Valley.
The landscaping includes drought-tolerant plants native to the region, with ornamental aluminum and precast concrete fencing surrounding the site.

The temple's exterior is finished with white marble from Torreón, Mexico, featuring a single attached spire topped with an Angel Moroni statue.

facts: The Twin Falls Idaho Temple is the 128th operating temple of the Church and the fourth temple built in Idaho.

The temple site covers 9.1 acres (3.7 hectares) with a total floor area of 31,245 square feet (2,903 square meters) and stands 152 feet 10 inches (46.6 meters) tall at an elevation of 3,676 feet (1,121 meters).

The exterior features 282 precast concrete panels with quartz rock finish, with individual panels weighing up to 30 tons, incorporating materials from around the world including wood from Africa, granite from India, and stone from Montana.

The temple showcases the Syringa flower motif in 200 art-glass windowpanes containing 12,000 individual glass pieces, and features murals by Idaho artist Leon Parson depicting local landscapes inspired by nearby Shoshone Falls.

During the 5-week public open house from July 11 to August 16, 2008, the temple welcomed 159,863 visitors (10,000 more than anticipated), with 40% from other faiths and supported by 650 daily volunteers.

The temple was built on the site of the former Candleridge Golf Course and resulted from a joint request by 14 stake presidents in the Magic Valley region who united in their desire for a temple in 1996.
The temple features a single attached end spire topped with an angel Moroni statue, three sealing rooms, two instruction rooms with two-stage progressive design, and one baptistry.

First Latter-day Saint families arrived in the Eugene area in 1909, and by 1930, Church membership in Oregon had grown to 3,230, influenced by timber industry workers and university populations.

facts: The Winchester Virginia Temple will be the second temple constructed in Virginia, following the Washington D.C. Temple (which serves Virginia but is located in Maryland).
The temple features a single attached tower design with 30,000 square feet (2,787 square meters) of total floor area on an 11.27-acre site.
Located approximately 75 miles west of Washington, D.C., at an elevation of 820 feet (250 meters).
Virginia is home to over 97,000 Latter-day Saints in more than 215 congregations, with additional temples planned for Roanoke and Norfolk.
The temple will include two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry to serve the growing Latter-day Saint population in the Shenandoah Valley region.

name: Winnipeg Manitoba Temple
address: 15 Centre St, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3Y 2C3, Canada
facts: First temple built in the province of Manitoba
9th temple built in Canada and 169th operating temple of the Church
Built on a 7.7-acre (3.1 hectares) site with 16,100 square feet total floor area
Features a 105-foot tall (32.0 meters) single attached end spire topped with an angel Moroni statue
Exterior design features red brick with limestone decorative elements, inspired by early Winnipeg churches and the Copenhagen Denmark Temple
Contains original art glass windows depicting the prairie crocus, Manitoba's provincial flower
Interior includes one instruction room, one sealing room, and one baptistry
Construction was prophesied by President Gordon B. Hinckley during his 1998 tour of Canada
Dedicated during the COVID-19 pandemic, becoming the first temple dedicated in 20 months
Design incorporates architectural elements from historic small country churches found throughout the Canadian prairies

name: Winter Quarters Nebraska Temple
address: 8283 N 34th St, Omaha, NE 68112
facts: First temple built in Nebraska and the 104th dedicated temple of the Church
Located adjacent to a Church-owned pioneer cemetery in Historic Winter Quarters, Florence, commemorating the Mormon pioneers' difficult westward journey
One of only three temples built on locations of historic significance for the early Church, alongside the Palmyra New York and Nauvoo Illinois temples
Sister building to Snowflake Arizona Temple, sharing the same core architectural plan
Features Bethel white granite exterior with a single attached spire topped by an Angel Moroni statue, standing 86 feet tall
Compact design with 16,000 square feet of floor area on a 1.92-acre site, containing two instruction rooms (two-stage progressive), two sealing rooms, and one baptistry
Dedicatory services were broadcast via the Church's encrypted satellite system to stake centers across North America
Located at an elevation of 1,158 feet near the Mormon Trail Center at Historic Winter Quarters

name: Yigo Guam Temple
address: 321 Chalan Pale Ramon Lagu, Rte 1, Yigo, Guam 96929
facts: First temple in Micronesia and the 172nd operating temple of the Church
Site covers 5.8 acres (2.3 hectares) at an elevation of 487 feet (148 meters)
Total floor area of 6,861 square feet (637 square meters) with a height of 74 feet (22.6 meters)
Features a single attached end tower with exterior of painted hand-troweled stucco and Moleanos limestone
Architectural design inspired by local Chamorro latte stone architecture, connecting the temple to Guam's indigenous heritage
Contains one instruction room, one sealing room, and a baptistry
Located near Andersen Air Force Base in northern Guam
Damaged by Typhoon Mawar on May 24, 2023, requiring two months of closure for repairs before reopening

name: Yorba Linda California Temple
address: 17130 Bastanchury Rd, Yorba Linda, California 92886, United States
facts: Features Spanish Mission design with a single attached end tower and white Blanco Macael marble exterior accented with pink and gray Rosa Porrino granite
Constructed using structural steel and insulated concrete forms on a 5.46-acre site with a total floor area of 30,872 square feet
Contains two instruction rooms, two sealing rooms, and one baptistry, with custom art glass featuring palm elements throughout
Interior features original artwork celebrating Southern California, decorative 24-karat gold leaf accents, and Murano glass fixtures in the bride's room
The ninth temple in California and second in Orange County, standing 70 feet tall at an elevation of 361 feet

name: Yuma Arizona Temple
address: East 36th Street and South 8 East Avenue, Yuma, Arizona

timeline.by: President Russell M. Nelson at 194th Annual General Conference

facts: Will be the 7th temple in Arizona, following Mesa (1927), Phoenix (2014), Gilbert (2014), Tucson (2017), Payson (2015), and others
Site size: 7 acres (2.8 hectares)
Total floor area: 18,500 square feet (1,719 square meters)
Features a single attached end tower with single-story design
Temple site elevation: 210 feet (64 meters) above sea level
Located at the corner of East 36th Street and South 8 East Avenue in Yuma
At time of announcement, Arizona had over 440,000 Latter-day Saints in approximately 925 congregations
Latter-day Saints have been present in Arizona since the 1850s when early explorers first visited the region
